Name: dynamic_kv_cache_reconfiguration
Title: Dynamic KV-Cache Layout Reconfiguration for Efficient LLM Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamically reconfiguring the KV-cache layout based on runtime memory access patterns can significantly reduce memory-bandwidth bottlenecks and improve tensor-core occupancy, leading to measurable latency and throughput improvements in LLM inference.
Related Work: Existing work on LLM inference optimization focuses on static KV-cache layouts (e.g., AMP-ViT, SNIP) or uniform quantization strategies (e.g., EMQ). These approaches often fail to adapt to the dynamic memory access patterns observed during inference, leading to suboptimal hardware utilization. HiLoRA introduces hierarchical routing but does not address the KV-cache layout's impact on memory bandwidth. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a dynamic reconfiguration mechanism that adapts to runtime conditions, validated through empirical profiling.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) suffer from memory-bandwidth bottlenecks during inference due to inefficient KV-cache layouts. This paper introduces a dynamic KV-cache reconfiguration mechanism that adapts to runtime memory access patterns, optimizing memory-bandwidth utilization and tensor-core occupancy. We propose a lightweight profiler that monitors memory access patterns and dynamically adjusts the KV-cache layout to minimize stalls and maximize hardware efficiency. Our experiments on a commodity GPU demonstrate significant improvements in latency (up to 30%) and throughput (up to 25%) for popular LLMs, validated through detailed Nsight traces and roofline analysis. This work bridges model compression with deployment systems engineering, providing a practical solution for efficient LLM inference.
Experiments: Baseline profiling: Measure latency, throughput, and memory-bandwidth utilization for static KV-cache layouts on a commodity GPU using Nsight traces.
Dynamic reconfiguration: Implement a lightweight profiler that monitors memory access patterns and adjusts the KV-cache layout dynamically. Measure latency, throughput, and hardware utilization.
A/B testing: Compare the dynamic reconfiguration approach against static layouts under fixed accuracy budgets, reporting p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
Hardware analysis: Collect detailed traces (occupancy, achieved bandwidth, warp stall reasons) to attribute gains to specific hardware causes.
Scalability testing: Evaluate the approach on multiple GPUs/driver versions and report variance across runs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Dynamic reconfiguration may introduce overhead that outweighs the benefits in certain scenarios.
The lightweight profiler may not capture all memory access patterns accurately.
The approach may not generalize to all LLM architectures or hardware configurations.
Scalability to larger models or multi-GPU setups requires further investigation.
